serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Elish Bhaja consists of one to two pan-fried hilsa fish pieces weighing approximately 150 g, served alongside rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Hilsa fish (Tenualosa ilisha), raw and fried preparation reference
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides nutrient data for hilsa fish (Tenualosa ilisha). Values for pan-fried hilsa are adapted from the raw hilsa profile with adjustment for frying in mustard oil (approximately 5–8 g fat absorbed per 100 g cooked fish), consistent with ICMR-NIN frying retention factors. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. Sodium adjusted upward to reflect salting during preparation. USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 175168, Shad, American, raw) used as a cross-reference for fatty acid profile and micronutrients where ICMR-NIN data are not reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — fish and seafood category; analogous estimate for pan-fried fish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Elish Bhaja (fried hilsa fish). GI estimated at 30 based on the University of Sydney GI Database category for plain fried fish and oily fish preparations, which consistently fall in the GI range of 25–40 due to negligible carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 3.15) / 100 ≈ 0.9. This is consistent with the low-GI classification for high-protein, low-carbohydrate fish dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from salting during preparation
hypertension.guidance: A 150 g serving of Elish Bhaja provides approximately 465 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid additional salt or high-sodium condiments, keeping total daily sodium below 2000 mg as per clinical guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Elish Bhaja has a glycemic index of approximately 30 and only 2.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it a suitable high-protein, low-glycemic option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments and prepared with minimal added oil.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High omega-3 polyunsaturated fat content from hilsa fish, offset by frying fat and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Hilsa fish is naturally rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are cardioprotective; however, pan-frying increases total fat and caloric density, and the sodium content warrants caution, so individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer smaller portions and consider steaming or grilling as lower-fat alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Elish Bhaja is a traditional Bengali pan-fried fish dish made from hilsa (Tenualosa ilisha), one of the most prized and culturally significant fish in the Indian subcontinent, particularly in West Bengal (India) and Bangladesh. The dish is prepared by marinating hilsa fish pieces in turmeric and salt, then shallow- or pan-frying them in mustard oil until golden and crisp on the outside while remaining moist and flaky inside. Onion slices and green chillies are often added during frying to impart aromatic depth. The hilsa fish has a distinctive rich, oily, and slightly sweet flavour with a tender, flaky texture and numerous fine bones. It is a high-protein, high-fat dish due to the natural oil content of hilsa and the frying process. Nutritionally, it provides approximately 235 kcal per 100 g, 21.8 g protein, and 15.5 g fat, including beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (EPA and DHA) that support cardiovascular health. The carbohydrate content is very low at 2.1 g per 100 g, giving it a low glycemic index of approximately 30, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose levels. Sodium content is moderate at approximately 310 mg per 100 g due to salting during preparation, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Elish Bhaja is typically served as a main course alongside steamed rice (bhaat) and is a staple of Bengali festive and everyday meals. In Singapore, it is served in Indian and Bengali restaurants as a premium seafood dish. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free.

Is hilsa fish good for diabetes?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for pan-fried hilsa exists; nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN raw hilsa data with frying adjustment factors. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database fish category. Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central for fatty acid and micronutrient profile. Medium confidence is appropriate given the adaptation rather than direct measurement.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (hilsa fish, raw) with frying oil absorption estimated at approximately 5–8 g fat per 100 g cooked fish using standard ICMR-NIN frying retention factors. 2. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database fish and seafood category; no direct GI measurement for Elish Bhaja was found in the literature. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): mustard oil and hilsa fish contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; any naturally occurring trans fats are below detection threshold — confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA reference data. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value provided from USDA cross-reference for American shad, FDC ID 175168). 5. Sodium value of 310 mg per 100 g reflects salting during preparation; raw hilsa sodium is approximately 60–80 mg per 100 g per ICMR-NIN. 6. Vitamin D content of 8.5 mcg per 100 g is consistent with hilsa being one of the richest natural dietary sources of vitamin D among Indian fish species, per ICMR-NIN and published literature. 7. The restaurant source (Indian House/Dumpukht Grills & Curry, Boat Quay, Singapore) is noted for provenance but not used as a nutritional source. 8. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302051 and FoodEx2 code A0CJW are used as closest_parent matches; no exact fried preparation ontology codes exist in either system. 9. All -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with numeric estimates from ICMR-NIN and USDA cross-reference sources; no -1 sentinel values remain in this record.
